summary: Charles de Gaulle was the French general and statesman who led the Free French resistance from London during World War II, delivering the Appeal of 18 June, before returning to power amid the 1958 Algerian crisis to found and become the first president of France's Fifth Republic. His presidency ended the Algerian War through the Evian Accords (surviving repeated OAS assassination attempts as a result), pursued an independent, anti-American foreign policy that produced the Empty Chair Crisis and a veto of Britain's entry into the European Economic Community, and included his declaration of "Vive le Quebec libre!" during a visit to Montreal. He nearly lost power to the May 1968 protests and ultimately resigned in 1969 after losing a referendum on Senate reform.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: De Gaulle is identified in giveaways as the wartime leader of the Free French during World War II who became the founder and first president of France's Fifth Republic.

clues.clue: Wartime leader of the Free French during World War II who became founder and first president of France's Fifth Republic
examples: name this founder of the Fifth Republic and commander of the Free French in World War II.
name this wartime leader of the Free French, who became the first president of the Fifth Republic.

works.name: World War II & the Free French
works.description: As a young officer de Gaulle fell out with a mentor over the ghostwriting of a book, was captured at the Battle of Verdun, and later advocated mechanized warfare in his treatise The Army of the Future; after France's 1940 capitulation he broadcast the BBC's Appeal of 18 June asserting "France is not alone," led the Free French resistance, opened the Brazzaville Conference on colonial reform, and after Liberation used the punishment of "national degradation" against collaborators, though he was denied a Te Deum at Notre Dame because he was not yet a legitimate head of state.

clues.clue: Delivered the BBC-broadcast "Appeal of 18 June" the day after France's capitulation to Germany, asserting "France is not alone," urging resistance, and opposing the armistice overtures of Philippe Petain's new government
examples: This man delivered the "Appeal of 18 June" speech the day after France's capitulation to Germany, in which he asserted that "France is not alone" and urged the French to resist the German occupation.
In a speech broadcast on the BBC that few people heard, this politician declared that the flames of resistance "must not be extinguished and will not be extinguished".

clues.clue: As a young officer, fell out with a mentor over the final chapter of a book he was ghostwriting for him, "The Soldier"; after being captured at the Battle of Verdun, shared a POW cell with Mikhail Tukhachevsky at Ingolstadt
examples: This man fell out with a mentor over the last chapter of a book he was ghostwriting for his mentor, "The Soldier." After being captured at the Battle of Verdun, this soldier shared a cell with Mikhail Tukhachevsky at Ingolstadt.

clues.clue: Advocated for a mechanized military force in his treatise The Army of the Future, criticizing reliance on the Maginot Line
examples: This man advocated for a mechanized force in The Army of the Future and asked his troops to remain with him in the BBC-broadcasted Appeal of 18 June.
This officer criticized the French military for building the Maginot Line instead of developing a professional armored force in his treatise The Army of the Future.

clues.clue: Opened the Brazzaville Conference, which granted territorial assemblies and legal equality to colonial subjects
examples: This man opened a conference that granted territorial assemblies and legal equality to colonial subjects at Brazzaville.

clues.clue: As head of the Provisional Government after Liberation, used a punishment called "national degradation" against people found guilty of "national unworthiness"
examples: This man's government used a punishment called "national degradation," which was meted out to people found guilty of "national unworthiness."

clues.clue: In 1944, allegedly walked down the central aisle of Notre Dame while being shot at by snipers, but since he only led the Provisional Government and was not a legitimate head of state, he was honored with a Magnificat instead of a Te Deum
examples: However, since this man only led the Provisional Government of the French Republic and was not a legitimate head of state, this man was honored with a Magnificat instead.

works.name: Algerian War & the Evian Accords
works.description: De Gaulle ended the Algerian War of Independence by signing the Evian Accords, which granted Algeria its independence but were fiercely opposed by the pieds-noirs and the paramilitary OAS.

clues.clue: Signed the Evian Accords, which granted Algeria its independence and were fiercely opposed by the OAS
examples: The Evian Accords granted Algeria independence during this man's Presidency.
This leader angered Lester Pearson at Expo 67 by declaring "Long live free Quebec!" and earlier brokered Algerian independence in the Evian Accords.

works.name: Assassination Attempts (OAS)
works.description: De Gaulle survived repeated assassination attempts by the OAS after signing the Evian Accords, most famously the Petit-Clamart ambush on his Citroen DS, and refused clemency to organizer Jean Bastien-Thiry, the engineer of the Nord SS.10 missile who became the last person executed by firing squad in France; the attempts inspired Frederick Forsyth's novel The Day of the Jackal.

clues.clue: Survived an OAS ambush on his Citroen DS ("the Goddess"), which was sprayed with bullets and had two tires blown out; he tersely ordered his driver to speed up
examples: This man tersely ordered the driver of his Citroen DS limousine to drive faster while it was sprayed with bullets during an assassination attempt by the OAS.
This act was attempted by the engineer who developed the Nord SS.10 anti-tank missile, but he was foiled when a black car nicknamed the Goddess got away despite having two blown-out tires.

clues.clue: Refused clemency to organizer Jean Bastien-Thiry, who became the last person executed by firing squad in France
examples: This leader refused clemency to a captured assassin after the assassin stated that all that was needed to capture him was to "take away his spectacles and braces."
Jean Bastien-Thiry was the last person to be executed by firing squad in France for an attempted assassination of this leader after he signed the Evian Accords giving Algeria its independence.

clues.clue: An attempt on his life, orchestrated by Raoul Salan, inspired the plot of Frederick Forsyth's novel The Day of the Jackal
examples: The Day of the Jackal fictionalizes an assassination attempt on this leader by Raoul Salan's OAS.
An attempt on this leader's life inspired the plot of Frederick Forsyth's novel The Day of the Jackal.

clues.clue: The Petit-Clamart plot was codenamed Operation Charlotte Corday, intended to avenge the failure of the Generals' Putsch
examples: That plan was codenamed Operation Charlotte Corday and tried to avenge the failure of the Generals' Putsch.

clues.clue: An earlier plot was stopped at the "Battle of the Transistors," when radio messages coordinating men based at Bab-el-Oued were intercepted
examples: An earlier attempt to perform this act was stopped at the "Battle of the Transistors," when radio messages were intercepted to discredit men based at Bab-el-Oued.

clues.clue: The best-known plotting group was a club called the Old Headquarters (or Old Staff), which included two Hungarians, Gyula Sari and Lajos Marton
examples: The best-known group to attempt this act were members of a club called the Old Headquarters, or Old Staff, including two Hungarians Gyula Sari and Lajos Marton.

clues.clue: Appeared on television in an army uniform to help foil a Generals' putsch against him
examples: This leader appeared on television wearing an army uniform to foil the Generals' putsch against him.

works.name: European Integration, NATO & Economic Policy
works.description: De Gaulle clashed repeatedly with France's Common Market partners and allies, withdrawing representatives from the Hallstein Commission in the "Empty Chair Crisis," vetoing Britain's entry into the European Economic Community twice, pulling French forces from NATO's integrated command, and exchanging France's dollar reserves for gold in protest of the United States' "exorbitant privilege" under Bretton Woods.

clues.clue: Withdrew French representatives from the Hallstein Commission over a Common Agricultural Policy dispute, precipitating the "Empty Chair Crisis" and forcing the Luxembourg Compromise
examples: That withdrawal of a representative from the Hallstein Commission by this leader was the "empty chair crisis."
This leader's withdrawal of representatives from the Hallstein Commission precipitated the "empty chair crisis" and forced the Luxembourg Compromise.

clues.clue: Twice vetoed the United Kingdom's application to join the European Economic Community, under both Harold Macmillan's and Harold Wilson's governments
examples: The United Kingdom's initial application to join the European Communities was vetoed by this man who withdrew his country's military from NATO in 1966.
The governments of Harold MacMillan and Harold Wilson were both vetoed entry into the European Economic Community by this president.

clues.clue: Withdrew France's military forces from NATO's integrated command in 1966
examples: this man who withdrew his country's military from NATO in 1966.

clues.clue: His finance minister criticized the "exorbitant privilege" granted the United States by the Bretton Woods system, leading him to exchange France's dollar reserves for gold
examples: This politician's finance minister criticized the "exorbitant privilege" granted to the United States by the Bretton Woods system, resulting in this leader exchanging his country's dollar reserves for gold.
The phrase is frequently attributed to this President of France at the time, a former World War II hero who led France for almost all of the 1960s.

clues.clue: An ambassador of his proposed creating an organization called "the Union" in the Fouchet Plan
examples: An ambassador of this leader proposed the creation of an organization called "the Union" in the Fouchet Plan.

clues.clue: At a press conference discussing an arms embargo on Israel, called the Jewish people "elite, full of themselves and domineering"
examples: During a press conference discussing an arms embargo on Israel, this leader called the Jewish people "elite, full of themselves and domineering."

clues.clue: Clashed with Joao Goulart's Brazil over how lobsters move along the sea floor
examples: This world leader clashed with Joao Goulart's Brazil over how lobsters move in the sea.

works.name: Quebec Visit & "Vive le Quebec libre"
works.description: During a 1967 visit to Montreal for Expo 67, de Gaulle declared "Vive le Quebec libre!" from the city hall balcony, a statement widely read as support for Quebec separatism that angered Canadian Prime Minister Lester Pearson.

clues.clue: Declared "Vive le Quebec libre!" ("Long live free Quebec!") in an informal speech from Montreal's City Hall balcony during Expo 67, angering Prime Minister Lester Pearson
examples: This president angered Lester Pearson when he declared during a visit to Montreal "Vive le Quebec libre!"
This foreign politician angered Pearson by declaring "Long live Free Quebec" in Montreal during Expo 67.

works.name: Diplomatic Rhetoric & Notable Quotes
works.description: De Gaulle was known for pointed, quotable remarks in speeches and press conferences, from an ambiguous 1958 address in Algeria to jabs at the Soviet Union, at a rival Western leader, and at France's own culinary diversity.

clues.clue: During a speech in an Algerian city, used a language's ambiguous inclusive "you" to claim "I understood you"
examples: While visiting a city in Africa, this politician used a language's inclusive you for an ambiguous speech claiming "I understood you".

clues.clue: In one speech, claimed that it was "Europe, from the Atlantic to the Urals" that would "decide the destiny of the world"
examples: In one speech, this politician claimed "it is Europe, from the Atlantic to the Urals" that would "decide the destiny of the world".

clues.clue: Insisted on referring to the Soviet Union as "Russia," viewing the nation as the fundamental unit of history
examples: This leader insisted on referring to the Soviet Union as Russia due to viewing the nation as the fundamental unit of history.

clues.clue: Enviously remarked "look at the rabble cheering the old bandit" after a visiting foreign leader received a warmer public reception than himself
examples: After a visiting foreign leader received a warm response, this politician enviously said "look at the rabble cheering the old bandit".

clues.clue: Once asked "how can you govern a country which has two hundred and forty-six varieties of cheese"
examples: This person once asked "how can you govern a country which has two hundred and forty-six varieties of cheese."

clues.clue: Was derisively compared to a "surprised bathing llama" by another Western leader, who had earlier undermined him during the Levant Crisis
examples: A different country's leader derisively compared this leader to a surprised bathing llama and previously undermined this leader during the Levant Crisis.

works.name: May 1968 Protests & Resignation
works.description: The May 1968 protests, which began with student unrest at the Sorbonne and spread into nationwide wildcat strikes, nearly toppled de Gaulle's government and forced him to briefly flee to Baden-Baden by helicopter; he returned to win a parliamentary majority, but resigned the presidency the following year after losing a 1969 referendum on Senate reform.

clues.clue: The May 1968 protests, beginning with student unrest over the occupation of the Sorbonne and involving the first nationwide wildcat strikes, forced de Gaulle to flee by helicopter to the spa town of Baden-Baden
examples: Although he's not Romanian, this leader fled his country in a helicopter during a crisis that the Grenelle agreements failed to resolve.
This head of state fled to the spa town of Baden-Baden in response to growing leftist protests in May 1968.

clues.clue: The Grenelle Agreements, which raised the minimum wage, were rejected by protesters during the unrest
examples: The Grenelle Agreements, which increased the minimum wage, were rejected during this event, which based many of its ideas from the pamphlet On the Poverty of Student Life.

clues.clue: Upon returning from Baden-Baden, announced new elections in which his party, the UDR, won France's first-ever absolute parliamentary majority
examples: Upon his return, he announced an election in which his party, the UDR, gained its country's first-ever absolute majority in Parliament.

clues.clue: Resigned the presidency in 1969 after losing a referendum to reform the Senate, a defeat aided by Valery Giscard d'Estaing's public campaign for a "no" vote; he was succeeded by Georges Pompidou
examples: Giscard publicly advocated voting "no" on a referendum by this man to reform the Senate, which helped bring about this man's resignation.
he was succeeded by George Pompidou after the wildcat strikes of May 1968.

works.name: Return to Power & Founding the Fifth Republic
works.description: De Gaulle returned to power in 1958 amid the Algerian crisis, ending a decade-long political retirement he called his "crossing of the desert," and founded France's Fifth Republic as its first president, overcoming opposition from Pierre Mendes France's Union of Democratic Forces.

clues.clue: Returned to power in 1958, ending a period of political retirement he called his "crossing of the desert," amid the Algiers uprising led by Raoul Salan and General Jacques Massu's Committee of Civil and Army Public Safety, which toppled the Fourth Republic's government
examples: 1958 marked the end of the "crossing of the desert" of this French politician, who became president again after over a decade of political retirement.

This leader of the Free French during World War II had to flee the country during the May 1968 events. Somewhat ironically, this man had come to power during the May 1958 crisis, amid the Algerian War of Independence.

clues.clue: Following his 1958 return, was opposed by Pierre Mendes France's Union of Democratic Forces, formed after Mendes France's own eight-month government fell
examples: Following his eight-month reign, Mendes France led the Union of Democratic Forces against this man, who returned to power during the 1958 Algerian unrest before founding France's Fifth Republic.

comprehensive_summary: Charles de Gaulle first fell out with a mentor over the ghostwriting of the book "The Soldier," was captured at the Battle of Verdun in World War I, and shared a POW cell with Mikhail Tukhachevsky at Ingolstadt; between the wars he advocated a mechanized military force in his treatise The Army of the Future, criticizing France's reliance on the Maginot Line. After France's 1940 capitulation to Germany, he broadcast the BBC's "Appeal of 18 June," asserting "France is not alone" and opposing the armistice overtures of Philippe Petain's new government, and went on to lead the Free French resistance and open the Brazzaville Conference granting territorial assemblies and legal equality to colonial subjects. As head of the Provisional Government after Liberation he imposed the punishment of "national degradation" on collaborators found guilty of "national unworthiness," and in 1944 was allegedly shot at by snipers while walking through Notre Dame, though as a non-legitimate head of state he was honored only with a Magnificat rather than a Te Deum. De Gaulle returned to power in 1958 amid the Algiers uprising led by Raoul Salan and General Jacques Massu's Committee of Civil and Army Public Safety, ending his decade-long "crossing of the desert" retirement, overcoming Pierre Mendes France's Union of Democratic Forces, and founding France's Fifth Republic as its first president. His presidency ended the Algerian War through the Evian Accords, which granted Algeria independence but provoked fierce opposition from the OAS; Jean Bastien-Thiry, the engineer of the Nord SS.10 missile, organized the Petit-Clamart ambush on de Gaulle's bulletproof Citroen DS ("the Goddess") as part of Operation Charlotte Corday, avenging the failed Generals' Putsch, and was refused clemency, becoming the last person executed by firing squad in France; an earlier plot involving the club known as the Old Headquarters (including Hungarians Gyula Sari and Lajos Marton) was stopped at the "Battle of the Transistors," and the attempts on de Gaulle's life, orchestrated by Raoul Salan, inspired Frederick Forsyth's novel The Day of the Jackal. In Europe, de Gaulle withdrew French representatives from the Hallstein Commission over the Common Agricultural Policy, precipitating the "Empty Chair Crisis" and forcing the Luxembourg Compromise; he twice vetoed the United Kingdom's application to join the European Economic Community under Harold Macmillan and Harold Wilson, pulled French forces from NATO's integrated command in 1966, and had an ambassador propose an organization called "the Union" in the Fouchet Plan. His finance minister decried the "exorbitant privilege" the United States enjoyed under Bretton Woods, prompting de Gaulle to exchange France's dollar reserves for gold, while he also clashed with Joao Goulart's Brazil over lobster migration and, at a press conference on an arms embargo on Israel, made remarks calling the Jewish people "elite, full of themselves and domineering." During a 1967 visit to Montreal for Expo 67, he declared "Vive le Quebec libre!" from the city hall balcony, angering Canadian Prime Minister Lester Pearson. De Gaulle was also known for pointed rhetoric elsewhere: an ambiguous 1958 Algiers speech claiming "I understood you," a vision of "Europe, from the Atlantic to the Urals," his insistence on calling the Soviet Union "Russia," an envious remark about "the old bandit" after a rival leader's warm reception, a comparison of himself to a "surprised bathing llama" by another Western leader who had undermined him during the Levant Crisis, and a complaint about governing a country with "two hundred and forty-six varieties of cheese." The May 1968 protests, beginning with student unrest at the Sorbonne and spreading into nationwide wildcat strikes, rejected the wage-raising Grenelle Agreements and forced de Gaulle to flee briefly by helicopter to Baden-Baden; he returned to call elections in which his party, the UDR, won France's first-ever absolute parliamentary majority, but resigned the presidency in 1969 after losing a referendum on Senate reform, a defeat aided by Valery Giscard d'Estaing's public "no" campaign, and was succeeded by Georges Pompidou.
recursive_suggestions: Assassination Attempts of Charles de Gaulle — has its own tossup answerline (2021 ACF Nationals) with dense clue presence covering the Petit-Clamart ambush, Jean Bastien-Thiry, and Operation Charlotte Corday; candidate for a dedicated Event topic
May 1968 Protests in France — has its own tossup answerline (2013 NASAT) and heavy bonus presence beyond de Gaulle's biography; candidate for a dedicated Event topic

Evian Accords / Algerian War of Independence — has its own bonus-part answerline (2018 Penn Bowl) with content (First Indochina War, Pierre Mendes France, Mers El Kebir) largely excluded here; could support its own topic

Lester Pearson — recurring Canadian Prime Minister answerline (2019 ACF Regionals) with independent content (Suez Crisis mediation, Vietnam War remarks); excluded here except where directly about de Gaulle
Valery Giscard d'Estaing — recurring French President answerline (2012 QUARK) with independent bonus presence; distinct successor topic
Jacques Chirac — recurring French President answerline (2012 QUARK); distinct successor topic
Francois Mitterrand — recurring answerline (Observatory Affair, 1965 election, "permanent coup d'etat" critique) distinct from de Gaulle
Suez Crisis — has its own bonus-part answerline (2019 ACF Regionals); only tangentially related via Pearson, excluded from this page
